1:
The witnesses agree on the bare facts and divide chiefly over the meaning of one word — night. The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene: Nicodemus is a genuine member of the Sanhedrin, and John, writing to prove Jesus the Messiah, deliberately marks a man who will reappear as a friend of Christ (John 7:50; John 19:39). Robertson reads the encounter against the close of the previous chapter: Nicodemus is one to whom Jesus, who knew what was in man (John 2:25), does entrust himself. Lightfoot, the outlier in method, fills the margin with the Talmud's Nicodemus Ben Gorion and his miraculously answered prayer for water.

The Latin fathers (gathered in the Catena with Augustine, Bede, and Haymo) hear the night theologically: it is the darkness of ignorance and of a faith not yet born again. Haymo presses the limit of his confession — he calls Jesus Rabbi, a Master sent from God, but does not see that he is God. Augustine numbers him among those who believed at the feast (John 2:23) yet were not regenerate, and so still came in the dark.

The Antiochene Chrysostom strikes the same note from the Greek side: held back by Jewish weakness and fear, Nicodemus approaches with only a human estimate of Christ. The Reformed voices turn it to a lesson on the heart: Calvin makes him a specimen of the unstable faith that miracles excite, a man whose own greatness and reputation dazzled him; Gill notes that the Pharisees, for all their corruption, were the soundest sect, which makes his coming the more remarkable.

Where the schools differ is motive. Most — the fathers with Calvin — weigh the night toward fear, shame, and ignorance. The Puritan Matthew Henry alone presses the kinder reading: it was also prudence and zeal, a busy ruler stealing the night to seek Christ when the day was crowded, and he draws the lesson that personal converse with faithful ministers about the soul is of great use (Malachi 2:7). Yet even Henry grants the darker possibility, that fear of the chief priests kept Nicodemus incognito; and he reminds us that though not many mighty are called, here was one in whom the grace of Christ subdued the proudest opposition — a man who, though he came by night now, would own Christ openly at the last (John 19:39). Gill notes too that the Pharisees, for all their enmity, held the resurrection and the doctrine of angels and spirits, which makes this ruler's approach the more striking.
2:
The voices converge on a single judgement: Nicodemus says something true but far too small. His confession — a teacher come from God, proved by miracles no man could do unless God were with him — is sincere, yet it stops at the threshold. The schools work out, in their different keys, why the right premise yields too low a conclusion.

The grammatical-historical commentators do the close reading. Robertson notes that Jesus was already in the public eye after the temple cleansing, so a ruler's private visit is itself remarkable. Barnes hears in the we a desire for fuller private instruction, and adds the practical lesson that men of rank are best reached alone. Lightfoot weighs the plural carefully: it may claim that the Sanhedrin could not well deny the miracles, or it may be no more than the common idiom for 'it is acknowledged.' All of them treat the signs as credentials — the seal that authenticates a messenger — while observing that to call Christ only a teacher is to mistake the King for a prophet.

The Reformed reading is sharper on the man. Calvin finds his very caution telling: dazzled by his own dignity and reputation, and secretly puffed up with a high opinion of his learning, he descends to the rank of a scholar only under cover of darkness, unwilling to lose any part of his standing. The honour he pays Christ is real, but it is the honour one teacher grants another, not the worship due the Son of God.

The Wesleyan voices are the most concise and the most generous to his intent. Wesley compresses the whole verse: he came through desire, but by night through shame; and the we know means 'even we rulers and Pharisees.' Clarke supposes a man burdened with weighty questions who chose the night not so much from fear as from a wish to have Jesus alone, free of the press of the crowd that filled the day. On any reading, the confession of v.2 is the doorway, not the house: Christ will at once lift him from what miracles prove to what only the new birth can reveal.
3:
Here Jesus answers not the words Nicodemus spoke but the desire behind them, and the witnesses concentrate on a single Greek word and a single great necessity. The grammatical-historical commentators make the crux anothen. Robertson lays out its range — 'from above,' 'from heaven,' 'from the first,' 'again' — and argues from the puzzle of v.4 that Nicodemus took it as a second physical birth, even though 'from above' is the deeper sense. Barnes sharpens the shock: the Jews used 'born again' of a Gentile becoming a proselyte by baptism, but never of a Jew, who was thought to hold the kingdom by birthright; so to require it of a ruler of Israel overturns his whole confidence. Lightfoot agrees that the phrase points to a birth from heaven.

The Reformed voices weigh the solemn doubled Verily. Calvin reads it as a deliberate arousing of a hearer who might otherwise listen as if thinking of something else, and finds in the saying the settled law of the kingdom: unless a man be renewed he can neither understand nor enter it. Gill presses the same necessity — without the Spirit's regenerating grace none can see the kingdom of God.

The Wesleyan reading is the most existential. Wesley insists that no external profession, no ceremonial privilege, and no birthright can entitle anyone to the Messiah's kingdom: there must be an entire change of heart and life, wrought in man only by the almighty power of God, before he can see — that is, experience and enjoy — that kingdom. Clarke adds that the doubled amen carried the weight of an oath among the Jews, so the demand is laid down with the utmost gravity.

The lone Eastern voice, Cyril of Jerusalem, supplies the catechist's frame that anticipates v.5: since man is of twofold nature, soul and body, the cleansing is twofold — the Spirit sealing the soul, the water washing the body. The debate among them is narrow and friendly: whether anothen is best rendered 'from above' or 'again.' On the substance there is no division — a real, God-wrought second birth is the indispensable door to the kingdom.
4:
Nicodemus's incredulous question — how can a man be born when he is old? — becomes for the witnesses a study in spiritual obtuseness, and they read it with remarkable unanimity. The grammatical-historical commentators name it plainly. Robertson, borrowing Bernard's phrase, calls it 'this stupid misunderstanding,' and explains why a learned man could fall into it: Nicodemus was familiar with the notion of rebirth for a Gentile proselyte but had no category for a Jew needing to be reborn, and so he collapses the spiritual into the physical. Barnes makes the same point about the proselyte background, and Lightfoot traces the prejudice to its root — the Israelite's confidence that birth into Abraham's nation already secured him a place in the Messiah's kingdom. The second time of the question, Robertson notes, only heightens the grossness of the blunder.

The Antiochene Chrysostom reads the moment dramatically: hearing things too lofty for him, Nicodemus is darkened and reels like one on the point of falling from the faith, and he raises the objection of impossibility precisely to provoke a fuller explanation. Two marvels stagger him at once — such a birth and such a kingdom, neither yet heard of among the Jews.

The Reformed Calvin turns the verse into an indictment of the teachers: though the Law and the Prophets are full of renewal, the Scribes had so given themselves to useless subtleties that the very heart of piety — the grace of regeneration — was unknown to them, and Nicodemus is only the most visible instance.

The Wesleyan voices are brief and pointed. Clarke diagnoses the perennial fault: like the rest of the Jews and like multitudes of Christians, Nicodemus rested in the letter and the shadow without the thing signified, and our Lord's pointed address is aimed at exactly that condition. Wesley adds the human detail, taking when he is old as Nicodemus speaking of himself. The voices do not really debate here; they agree that the question exposes a mind still bound to the flesh, and that Christ's reply in v.5 meets it not with rebuke but with patient enlargement.
5:
This is the verse where the witnesses openly divide, and they divide over one word: water. The larger body — drawing on the grammatical-historical, Wesleyan, and Eastern schools — takes it of baptism. Barnes points to the apostolic usage of washing and renewal (Ephesians 5:26; Titus 3:5) and the cleansing promised in Ezekiel 36:25, calling baptism the sign and seal of the Spirit's renewing influence. Lightfoot and Clarke set it against proselyte baptism: the convert was washed as an emblem of a soul defiled by past sin, the outward water answering to the inward Spirit. Wesley keeps both together — the inward change and baptism as its outward sign and means, 'wherever baptism can be had.' The Eastern voices press this sacramentally: Cyril of Jerusalem makes the cleansing twofold, water for the body and Spirit for the soul, neither perfect without the other; Chrysostom calls it the begetting by baptism that enlightens the mind.

Against this stands the Reformed Calvin, who reviews the various readings and rejects a double rite. He takes water and Spirit as a single thing — the Spirit who purifies as water purifies — so that the phrase means 'born of the Spirit, who is like water.' On this view Christ describes one regeneration under a double figure, not baptism plus the Spirit. Robertson, characteristically, stands back: he records that 'many theories exist' for why Jesus adds of water here when v.7 and v.8 speak only of the Spirit, and he leaves the question open.

For all the division over the sign, the schools are one on the thing signified. None makes the water mechanical: even those who read it of baptism insist, with Cyril, that one must not think of 'the bare element' but look through it to the Spirit, and Barnes is explicit that not all who are baptized are saved. The cleansing that admits to the kingdom is the Spirit's; the debate is only whether water names the outward seal of that work or is itself a metaphor for it. Compare the washing of 1 Corinthians 6:11.
6:
With this verse the discussion turns from the necessity of the new birth to its ground in human nature, and the Reformed voices take the lead. Calvin reads it as an argument from contraries: the kingdom of God is shut against us unless we are spiritual, but from the womb we bring nothing except a carnal nature; therefore we are by birth banished from that kingdom and held under death until a second, spiritual begetting opens the door. Gill puts it plainly — man as born of the flesh is a mere natural man, carnal and corrupt, unable to discern or inherit spiritual things, so that being born again of the Spirit is a necessity, not an option.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the same line through the vocabulary. Barnes turns Nicodemus's attention to the natural condition of man: another physical birth, even were it possible, would only reproduce the same propensities and passions, so the needed change is of a different order. Robertson notes that the sharp contrast of flesh and Spirit already drawn in John 1:13 is recalled here precisely to rebuke the crudity of the question in v.4. Clarke states the governing principle — like begets like; the seed determines the plant, and the kingdom of God is spiritual.

The Wesleyan Wesley compresses it: flesh is mere flesh, void of the Spirit and even at enmity with it, while that which is born of the Spirit is spiritual, heavenly, divine — taking its character from its Author.

The Eastern and Catena voices lift the same truth toward grace and mystery. Chrysostom marvels that the Only-begotten has made us, who deserved punishment, into a new creature, echoing the apostle's word that whoever is in Christ is a new creation (2 Corinthians 5:17). Augustine, in the Catena, adds that the Spirit is invisibly present in Word and sacrament to accomplish this birth, so that the new birth is no work of ours but of the Spirit who alone can make a spiritual man. Gill underlines that the natural man, however refined, cannot discern spiritual things or inherit the kingdom until grace makes him spiritual; and Clarke states the law of seed and harvest — as is he who begat, so is he who is begotten of him — so that a hundred natural births could never mend the matter. There is no debate here, only one confession from many sides: what flesh transmits, only the Spirit can remake, and the necessity of the new birth in v.7 follows of course.
7:
This short verse is mostly a hinge, and the witnesses treat it as Christ steadying a staggered hearer while widening the demand to every man. The grammatical-historical commentators fasten on the change of number. Ellicott observes that the general laws of natural and spiritual generation just stated — that which is born — now receive a pointed personal application: 'Marvel not that I said unto thee (teacher as thou art) that ye (children of Abraham as ye are) must be born again.' Being Abraham's children after the flesh secures nothing, for the law of all human nature is that flesh rules; therefore the new birth is required of the whole nation, not merely of Gentile proselytes. Robertson catches the tense: 'do not begin to wonder,' an ingressive aorist, implying that Nicodemus had already started to marvel; and he notes that here Jesus repeats the point of v.3 with from above only, not yet adding of water. Barnes simply records that Nicodemus had probably betrayed fresh doubt, which Jesus now meets head-on.

The Reformed Calvin admits the verse has been 'tortured' by interpreters, but takes its plain thrust to be a reproof of Nicodemus's astonishment and a calm reassertion of the necessity already laid down: the wonder lies not in Christ's teaching but in the hearer's dullness.

The Wesleyan Wesley gives the verse its experiential definition: to be born again is to be inwardly changed from all sinfulness to all holiness, and it is fitly called a birth because as great a change then passes on the soul as passes on the body when it is born into the world. Ellicott presses the point home: in so far as they were children of Abraham according to the flesh, they were children of Abraham's sinful nature, and the law of that nature — that flesh rules — held them as it holds all mankind, so the demand is universal and inescapable. Gill adds that Nicodemus was astonished, the doctrine being altogether new and unheard of by him, which is why Christ steadies him before enlarging the theme.

The voices do not divide; they agree that Marvel not is both rebuke and invitation — Christ will not soften the law of the kingdom for rank or learning, yet he says it gently, to draw Nicodemus on rather than drive him off. The illustration of the wind in v.8 follows as the answer to his amazement.
8:
The wind-saying draws out the chapter's one sustained translation debate, and the grammatical-historical school carries both sides of it. Ellicott, in a long note, argues that the same word pneuma rendered 'Spirit' twice in this very verse should not be flattened into 'wind' in its first clause; he would read, 'the Spirit breatheth where He willeth, and thou hearest His voice,' hearing behind it the breath of life God breathed into man in Genesis 2:7. Robertson takes the opposite view on lexical grounds: pneuma occurs some 370 times in the New Testament and never means wind except in an Old Testament citation, while the verb pneo ('bloweth') always refers to wind; so 'wind' is the natural analogy. Barnes and Clarke read it as analogy too — the wind's effects are seen and felt though it is itself unseen and its course untraceable, and so it is with everyone born of the Spirit.

The Reformed voices dwell on what the analogy teaches: sovereignty. Gill calls the Spirit a free agent who works how, where, and when he pleases, and whose grace, like the wind, is powerful and irresistible, carrying all before it. Calvin guards the figure — there is no literal 'will' in the air, but its motion is free, uncertain, and variable, which is just the point: the Spirit's operation is as real and as perceptible in its effects as the wind, yet its manner is concealed, so that we are not to subject it to our reason.

The Wesleyan Wesley states the balance cleanly: the fact is plain — you are sure it blows — but the particular manner of its acting is past finding out; so the new birth is certain in its effects and inexplicable in its method. The Latin fathers in the Catena (Bede, Alcuin, with Augustine) press the doctrinal edge: the Spirit blows where He lists, choosing whose heart to visit, and the 'sound' you hear is the Word and Gospel in which He is invisibly present. Underneath the lexical dispute the schools are agreed — whether we read 'wind' or 'breath,' the lesson is the free, hidden, effectual working of the Spirit of God.
9:
Nicodemus's renewed 'How can these things be?' becomes for the witnesses a diagnosis of how a learned man fails before plain truth, and they read his question more in sorrow than in contempt. The grammatical-historical commentators expose the irony of his learning. Ellicott contrasts what the Rabbinic schools did know — the precise number of words, the shape of letters, the width of a fringe, the tithing of herbs — with what they could not grasp: 'spirit, life, a man's soul born again!' Barnes presses the inconsistency: men will admit difficult facts in nature yet stumble at parallel facts in religion, refusing a truth merely because they cannot explain its manner. Robertson is blunt — there are none so dull as those who will not see; preoccupation prevents insight, and Nicodemus's Pharisaic theology has left him almost proof against spiritual apprehension.

The Reformed Calvin locates the root in pride. Everything Nicodemus hears seems monstrous because he cannot understand its manner, and there is no greater obstacle than this wish to be wise beyond what is proper, which rejects with a kind of diabolical arrogance whatever is not explained to our reason — as if the infinite power of God could be limited to our poor capacity.

The Antiochene Chrysostom marks a turn in Christ's manner: because Nicodemus still clings, after clear evidence, to a low and carnal system, the Lord now addresses him with greater severity — yet, entering on high doctrines, he ever checks himself in compassion for the weakness of his hearer, and turns from the loftiest truths to more humble ones. The Latin Haymo, in the Catena, is gentlest of all: Nicodemus asks not to deny the fact and not in censure, but because he genuinely wishes to be informed, simply unable to take in the mysteries of the divine majesty. Clarke sharpens the warning for every age: many profess to believe only because they comprehend, but they are impostors who speak so, for there is not a man in the universe who can fully comprehend even one operation of God; and Gill observes that Nicodemus remained as ignorant as ever, though Christ had explained the new birth by water and the Spirit and illustrated it by the wind. The voices do not contend; together they sketch a sincere but proud mind, and Christ's reproof in v.10 follows directly.
10:
Christ's question — art thou the teacher of Israel, and knowest not these things? — turns the spotlight from Nicodemus's confusion to his office, and the grammatical-historical commentators make the most of the Greek article. Ellicott renders it 'the teacher of Israel,' emphatic, pointing either to Nicodemus as a teacher of repute or to the Sanhedrin he represents, and hears in it a just indignation: 'You who teach others, have you need to learn the very first lessons of true religion?' Barnes insists the doctrine was not new but clearly taught in the Old Testament, citing the prayer for a clean heart and a right spirit (Psalms 51:10) and the promise of a new heart and a new spirit (Ezekiel 36:26; compare Ezekiel 11:19). Robertson gives the memorable diagnosis: Nicodemus's theology has fixed him in 'the three terrible r's of mere traditionalism' — rote, rut, rot — so that the new truth lies outside his groove. Lightfoot, in his manner, sets a rabbinic parable of a wise man fooled by a boy's directions to illustrate learning that misses the plain road.

The Reformed Calvin reads the rebuke as aimed precisely at the man's pride. Christ sees that he is spending his pains to no purpose on so self-satisfied a hearer, and so he begins to reprove him sharply, censuring his ignorance in the very matter where he chiefly plumed himself on his acuteness — for he had thought that to deny a thing possible was a mark of gravity and intelligence.

The Wesleyan Clarke widens the reproof to every age: hast thou taken on thee to guide the blind, and yet knowest not the way thyself? He observes the irony that Nicodemus commanded proselytes to be baptized with water as an emblem of a new birth, while remaining a stranger to the cause, necessity, nature, and effects of that birth — and adds that there are masters in Israel still who publish the same ignorance. The voices agree without dispute: the failure is not of evidence but of a heart and a school content with the externals of religion.
11:
With a third solemn Verily Jesus moves from rebuke to the certainty of his message, and the witnesses concentrate on two things: the force of the plural we, and the gravity of rejecting attested truth. The Reformed Calvin reads the we broadly: Christ joins himself to all the prophets and servants of God, speaking in the person of them all. Where philosophers and vainglorious teachers parade trifles of their own invention, it is the mark of Christ and of every true minister that they deliver nothing uncertain — only what God has made known. Gill allows the same range, naming the Baptist, the disciples, and the Old Testament prophets who agreed in this testimony.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the unusual plural more cautiously. Ellicott notes that the plural is not Christ's usual idiom and that the immediate return to the singular forbids treating it as a mere plural of majesty; he takes Christ to join with himself those who have spoken, known, and testified, and whose witness the Jews have refused — pre-eminently the forerunner whose life-work was to bear witness to the Light. Barnes paraphrases it of Jesus and those engaged with him in preaching the gospel: 'We who spread this new doctrine speak what we know,' a claim he had every right to make. Robertson observes John's fondness for the doubled amen and for the verbs speak and bear witness, the perfect tense of 'we have seen' grounding the testimony in firsthand sight.

The Wesleyan Clarke turns the verse on the preacher's conscience: the words are a strong but delicate reproof to Nicodemus, who though a master of Israel did not understand the rudiments of salvation; and he adds the searching application that wretched is the minister who recommends to others a salvation he has not himself felt, trafficking in an unfelt truth.

The difference among them — whether the we embraces all the prophets or only Christ's immediate fellow-witnesses — is a difference of scope, not of substance. All agree that the closing complaint, ye receive not our witness, makes the Jews' unbelief the more inexcusable: the things are sure and clear, yet they will not believe, as v.12 goes on to press.
12:
The witnesses agree that the contrast here is not between the secular and the sacred but between truths whose stage is earth and truths whose stage is heaven, and that the failure to receive the lower bars the way to the higher. The grammatical-historical commentators are precise. Barnes insists the earthly things are not sensual or worldly but operations of the Spirit that occur on earth, whose effects are visible — the plainest of religious doctrines. Robertson glosses them as ta epigeia, things upon the earth like the work of the kingdom and the new birth, set against ta epourania, the things that take place in heaven; if Nicodemus cannot believe the former, how will he believe the latter? Ellicott adds that the new birth itself belongs to 'earthly things,' since it is the entrance to a life that, though spiritual, is still lived on earth.

The Antiochene Chrysostom turns the verse into a maxim about Christ's pedagogy, one he says he will not cease to repeat: when about to touch sublime doctrines, Jesus often restrains himself for the infirmity of his hearers and dwells on truths that partake of condescension rather than on those worthy of his greatness — the lofty saying, once uttered, is enough, but the lowly must be pressed.

The Reformed Calvin lays the blame on the hearer. Christ shows that the fault is not his that all are not instructed, since he stoops even to the lowest in order to raise us; it is the common fault of men to crave an ingenious and witty style and to hold the Gospel in less esteem because it does not sound lofty, so that many despise its plainness to their own loss.

The Wesleyan Wesley supplies the content the others leave general: the earthly things are such as the new birth and the present privileges of God's children; the heavenly things are such as the eternity of the Son and the unity of Father, Son, and Spirit. The voices do not divide; they agree that Christ has lisped to Nicodemus in his own language and that the obstacle is not the obscurity of the doctrine but the dullness of the heart — a theme v.13 answers by naming the one qualified Revealer.
13:
This verse lifts the discourse to Christology, and the witnesses read it as Christ's claim to be the one qualified Revealer of heavenly things. The grammatical-historical commentators ground that claim in his unique descent. Barnes argues that no one is fit to speak of heaven but he who has been there and returned; Enoch and Elijah were borne up, but only the Son came down, and so only he can teach what passes in heaven. Ellicott hears behind the words the proverb of Agur — 'Who hath ascended up into heaven, or descended?... what is his name, and what is his son's name?' (Proverbs 30:4) — now answered in Christ. Robertson presses the high doctrine: the participle marks the Incarnation of the pre-existent Son who was in heaven before he came down, with no allusion to the later Ascension, and the disputed clause which is in heaven, if genuine, only emphasizes his timeless existence.

The Reformed voices make the same point pastorally. Calvin urges Nicodemus not to trust his own sagacity, since no mortal can by his own powers enter heaven but only he who goes thither under the guidance of the Son of God; Christ alone has a pure knowledge of the mysteries of God. Gill adds that Enoch and Elijah did not ascend in the sense Christ means — as the only one who had been in heaven to bring its knowledge down.

The Latin Augustine, in the Catena, reads the verse mystically: the spiritual birth makes earthly men heavenly, which is possible only as they are made members of Christ, so that the one who ascends is, in a sense, Christ ascending in his members.

The sharpest exegetical interest gathers on the Son of man who is in heaven. Wesley takes it to prove omnipresence — he could not be in heaven and on earth at once unless his divine nature filled all things — and calls it 'a plain instance of the communication of properties,' whereby what belongs to the divine nature is spoken of the human and the reverse. Robertson notes the textual question but agrees that, if read, the clause asserts the divine nature's heavenly existence. The voices do not finally divide: all confess one person in two natures, the heavenly Son of Man who alone can speak the heavenly things of v.12.
14:
Christ now turns from the new birth to its source, and the witnesses read the brazen serpent of Numbers 21:8–9 as the chapter's first great type. The grammatical-historical commentators secure the link. Robertson draws the vivid parallel between Moses setting the serpent on the standard and the Son of Man who 'must' (dei, one of the heavenly things) be lifted up, noting that in John the verb hypsoo always points to the Cross (John 8:28; John 12:32). Ellicott presses the closeness of type and antitype — the poison-virus of the serpents answering to the venom of sin — while Barnes and Lightfoot fill in the wilderness story and its later Jewish embellishments.

The Reformed voices develop the likeness most fully. Gill catalogues the agreements: the serpent was of brass, the meaner metal, made in the likeness of a fiery serpent yet without poison, lifted on a pole — figuring Christ made in the likeness of sinful flesh yet without sin, made a curse, and lifted up. Calvin, however, opens the one real division of reading: he takes lifted up chiefly of Christ exalted and openly manifested to men of every class by the preaching of the Gospel, rather than narrowly of the cross — for to be lifted up means to be placed on high and exhibited to the view of all.

The Eastern Chrysostom, with the Catena fathers Bede and Augustine, reads the serpent as a figure of the Passion and of man's salvation, introducing the teacher of the Law to the spiritual sense of his own Scriptures.

The Wesleyan Clarke stands as the outlier: he frankly doubts that the brazen serpent was ever intended to be a type of Christ, warning that resemblances can be drawn out of anything; yet he holds the practical parallel — as the stung Israelite looked and lived, so the believing sinner looks to Christ and is healed. The debate, then, is real but bounded: whether 'lifted up' means first the cross (most) or the public exaltation by preaching (Calvin), and whether the serpent is a designed type (Gill, the fathers) or only an apt illustration (Clarke). All agree that the look of faith, met in v.15, is the appointed cure.
15:
The application of the serpent-type now lands on faith and life, and the witnesses read it for the freeness of the offer and the nature of the gift. The grammatical-historical commentators attend first to the text. Ellicott and Clarke note that the words not perish, but have been added here from v.16; omitting them, the sentence should run 'that every one who believeth may have in him eternal life,' with the trust resting on Christ as its object. Robertson takes the same construction, joining in him to have rather than to believeth, and dwells on the gift: eternal life, the phrase John couples with zoe sixteen times in the Gospel, is more than endless — it is a present sharing in the very life of God in Christ, begun now and lasting forever (compare John 5:26; John 17:3). Barnes stresses the universality of whosoever: all may come, all who feel themselves sinners and look to him as their only Saviour.

The Wesleyan voices keep the serpent-figure in view. Wesley draws the parallel exactly: he must be lifted up that he might purchase salvation for all believers, and all who look to him by faith recover spiritual health, even as all who looked at that serpent recovered bodily health. The look is the whole of it — not a work, but a turning of the eye to the appointed remedy.

The Reformed Gill guards the quality of that faith. It is not a mere historical or temporary assent to facts about Christ's person and office, but a faith by which the soul sees a glory, fullness, and suitableness in him as a Saviour, goes to him, ventures upon him, lays hold of him, and relies on him — provided, he says, the faith be 'of the right kind.'

The lone Eastern voice, Cyril of Jerusalem, gives the catechist's summons that ties this verse to the next: believe on Jesus Christ, Son of the living God, the Only-begotten, according to the Gospel — that whoever believes on him should not perish but have everlasting life. The voices do not contend; they agree that the cure for the serpent's bite is a single, trusting look, and that the life it brings is nothing less than God's own.
16:
On the chapter's most famous verse the witnesses are at once united in adoration and divided over its reach. The grammatical-historical commentators frame its scale. Robertson calls it the 'Little Gospel,' not a quotation from Jesus but John's own inspired and marvellous summary of the mission of the Lord, in which vv.16–21 recapitulate the teaching to Nicodemus. Barnes draws the careful distinction that will govern the debate: God's love here is not the love of complacency, which approves conduct, but the love of benevolence, which desires the sinner's happiness — he hates the sin yet loves the sinner. Ellicott confesses that perhaps no verse has been so much explained and so little explained, its only limit being humanity itself.

The great division concerns the world. The Reformed Gill argues that not every individual is meant, since not all are objects of God's special love nor is Christ given to all; he reads 'the world' of the elect among both Jews and Gentiles. Calvin keeps the stress on the fountain: Christ opens up the first cause of our salvation, the unmerited love of God, so that our minds may find calm repose; and he too presses the universality of the whosoever, that no one might think himself excluded. The Wesleyan voices press the widest reading. Wesley insists 'the world' is all men under heaven, even those who despise his love and finally perish — otherwise, he reasons, their unbelief would be no sin, for they could not be blamed for failing to believe that Christ was given for them. Clarke adds that the little word so puts an eternity of meaning into the verse, a love no tongue can describe.

The Eastern Chrysostom reads it pastorally against fear: Christ has just spoken of being lifted up to death, so lest the hearer be cast down he adds that the One given up to death was the Son of God, whose death would be the source of life eternal — marvel not, then, at the cross. The Catena's Alcuin and Augustine carry the thought into v.18, that he who believes and cleaves to Christ as a member to the head is not condemned. Cyril of Jerusalem simply gathers it into the creed. Beneath the real Calvinist–Arminian division over the world, all confess one wonder: that the holy God should so love a guilty world as to give his only-begotten Son, drawing on the same apostle's words in 1 John 4:10 and Romans 5:8.
17:
The witnesses read this verse as the explanation and safeguard of the love just declared: the errand of the Son is salvation, not condemnation. The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen the key word. Ellicott notes that krino means originally to separate, and in the moral sense to separate good from evil; 'condemn' gives the English reader a stronger impression than the Greek, so 'judge' is the better rendering. Lightfoot supplies the Jewish background that gives the verse its edge: in the rabbinic schools 'the world' often meant the Gentile nations, whom many expected the Messiah to destroy; Clarke makes the same point and concludes that Christ here corrects that false hope, teaching a doctrine of salvation for the Gentile world, not its ruin. Barnes adds the balance that, though Jesus did not come then to condemn, the time is coming when he will return to judge the living and the dead (Acts 17:31; 2 Corinthians 5:10).

The Reformed Calvin treats the verse as a confirmation of the preceding: it was not in vain that God sent his Son; since he came not to destroy, it follows that it is the Son's peculiar office that men should obtain salvation by him. The word world is repeated, Calvin observes, precisely that no man should think himself wholly excluded while he keeps the way of faith open.

The Antiochene Chrysostom guards the verse from abuse. Some, he says, take God's lovingkindness as a license, saying there is no hell and no future punishment; against such he sets the Scripture that mercy and wrath both come from God, and his indignation rests upon sinners. The mission of mercy in this verse does not cancel the judgment that v.18 and v.19 will name; it defers it and offers escape.

The Wesleyan Wesley states the purpose simply — God sent his Son to save, not to condemn, the world — adding, with a glance at his opponents, 'although many accuse him of it.' The voices do not divide here; together they hold the saving design and the real, future judgment in one frame, so that the gospel of mercy is neither cheapened into indulgence nor narrowed to one nation.
18:
The witnesses read this verse as the hinge between salvation and judgment, and the grammatical-historical commentators make everything turn on the tenses. Ellicott marks the change the older versions blur: the believer is not judged, present and continuing, while the unbeliever hath been — and is — already judged, perfect tense; the sentence does not wait for a future assize but stands the moment the revelation of God in his Son is refused. Robertson agrees: trust in Christ prevents condemnation because he takes our place and pays the penalty (Romans 8:32), so the believer does not come into judgment (John 5:24), while judgment has already passed on the one who will not come to Christ for life (John 5:40). Barnes grounds the acquittal in the substitution: Jesus died in our stead, and by his sufferings our sins are expiated, so that God may consistently pardon.

The Reformed voices dwell on the comfort of it. Gill explains why the believer is not condemned: he is openly in Christ, and the sentence of death that fell on all in Adam has been executed already upon Christ his surety, so that his death is the believer's security from all condemnation. Calvin draws out the pastoral fruit — Christ so frequently and earnestly repeats that all believers are beyond the danger of death because the conscience needs a firm and assured confidence, not to be kept perpetually trembling; the present tense, in Hebrew fashion, means that believers are now safe from the fear of condemnation.

The Wesleyan voices press both edges. Wesley reads 'is not condemned' as 'is acquitted, is justified before God,' and takes the name of the only-begotten Son as standing for the great and glorious person himself. Clarke turns to the unbeliever: when the gospel is preached and the way of salvation made plain, his refusal leaves him under the condemnation that already rests on all sinners, with this added — that he has slighted the only Son of God, which is the grossest insult to the divine majesty.

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem gathers the verse into the creed's promise: he that believes is not judged, but has passed out of death into life. The voices do not divide; together they hold that faith brings a present, settled acquittal, and that unbelief is not merely awaiting judgment but already under it, for the reason v.19 supplies.
19:
The witnesses read this verse as the answer to a natural complaint — why should unbelievers be condemned? — and they find the answer in the will, not in the intellect. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the light and trace the verdict. Barnes takes light peculiarly of the Messiah, the Christ often so named (Isaiah 9:2; Isaiah 60:1; compare Matthew 4:16), and makes this the very ground of condemnation: light has come, and men preferred darkness. Ellicott prefers 'judgment' to 'condemnation' and observes that the light makes the darkness visible — both were set before men, and that they chose darkness was the act of their own will, determined by the evil of their deeds; he hears the echo of John 1:5. Robertson calls it no arbitrary sentence but the working out of a moral law, the permanent result of the Incarnation already announced in the Prologue.

The Reformed Calvin meets the murmur directly: wicked men charge God with excessive rigour, but Christ shows that every man ought to impute the blame to himself, for unbelief is the testimony of a bad conscience, and it is their own wickedness that hinders them from approaching the light.

The Eastern Chrysostom heightens the guilt of the refusal: so far from seeking the light or labouring to find it, men had the light itself come to them, and they would not admit it — who can pity one who will not approach the light when it comes to him? He adds a sharp application: a pagan whose life matches his false gods is less condemned than a Christian who lives in wickedness against the light he has received. The Catena's Alcuin and Bede name Christ himself as that light.

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the precise conclusion: this is the reason any are finally lost — not simply that they came into the world with a corrupt nature, true as that is, nor merely that they lived in sin, but that they refused the salvation God sent. The voices do not divide; together they make the judgment self-incurred, a theme v.20 deepens by naming the hatred of the light.
20:
This verse states more strongly the diagnosis of v.19, and the witnesses, with one mind, locate unbelief in the moral will rather than the understanding. The grammatical-historical commentators do the close work. Robertson weighs the word phaulos, which moves from 'worthless' to 'wicked,' and draws the contrast he finds in John 5:29 between doing good things and practising evil things; the one who practises ill not only avoids the light but hates it, and so 'talks against it' — ridiculing Christ, Christianity, churches, and preachers in a tone of sheer ignorance. Barnes generalizes it to all wicked men: they choose to practise their deeds in darkness and are afraid of the light because they could be easily detected, which is why most crimes are committed at night; so the sinner hates the gospel, for it condemns his conduct and his conscience would trouble him if it were enlightened. Ellicott marks the escalation — not merely does the evildoer love darkness rather than light, he hates the light, whose presence makes manifest and reproves works he would hide even from himself.

The Reformed Calvin states the cause without remainder: the light is hateful to such men for no other reason than that they are wicked and desire, as far as they can, to conceal their sins. From this he draws the terrible irony that by rejecting the remedy they may be said purposely to cherish the very ground of their condemnation; and he warns against the self-deception that mistakes enmity to the gospel for godly zeal — those enraged against the light are not stirred by piety but abhor and shun it that they may flatter themselves more freely in their sins.

The Wesleyan Clarke adds his characteristic philology, tracing the word for evil to a root suggesting that which is rolled in dust, a token of the humiliation and vileness of such deeds; but he holds the same moral account. The voices do not differ. They agree that the gospel is not rejected because it is unclear or unproven but because it shines, and that the love of concealment is the secret engine of unbelief — over against which v.21 sets the one who does the truth and comes to the light.
21:
Over against the lover of darkness, this verse sets the one who 'doeth truth,' and the witnesses divide gently over how such a man can exist before grace. The grammatical-historical commentators describe him from the text. Barnes takes 'doing truth' as obeying the truth, opposed to the error and falsehood the sinner believes; such a man loves the truth and seeks it more, coming to the light by prayer and searching the Scriptures. Ellicott draws the moral portrait: with fixed purpose he does what remains morally true when every veil is removed, and though the light too will expose his sins and errors, he hates them the moment he knows them, cuts them from his life at any cost, and carries his whole being to the light. Robertson supplies the memorable image — such men are 'spiritual heliotropes,' drawn by the light, not driven from it; and he guards the last clause, that their deeds are not claimed to be perfect, only to have been wrought in the sphere and power of God.

That last clause raises the Reformed Calvin's difficulty: the statement seems to grant that some are upright and true before they are renewed by the Spirit, which would clash with the whole tenor of Scripture, since faith is the root from which good works spring. His solution is that 'to do truth' here means to acknowledge that we are miserable and destitute of all power to do good; that conviction of poverty, driving us to flee to the grace of Christ, is itself the true preparation for faith — so the verse describes not a pre-existing goodness but the first movement of grace. Gill stands with him, taking the doer of truth as one who acts from love to God and with a view to his glory.

The Wesleyan voices add the biographical seal. Wesley notes that even Nicodemus afterward 'came to the light,' and Clarke points to the sequel — Nicodemus defending Christ in the Sanhedrin (John 7:50) and helping to bury him (John 19:39) — so that this night-discourse bore its fruit. The debate is fine, not fierce: all agree the deeds are 'wrought in God,' the work of grace, not the ground of it.
22:
With the Nicodemus discourse closed, the narrative resumes, and the witnesses attend to its geography, its baptism, and its lesson of industry. The grammatical-historical commentators clarify the movement. Robertson and Ellicott explain that the land of Judea means the country districts outside Jerusalem — the same distinction drawn in Acts 1:8 — into which Jesus withdrew to teach and to gather converts among the country people. All of them, with Barnes, Clarke, and Wesley, insist on the qualification John himself will make: Jesus did not personally administer baptism but baptized through his disciples (John 4:2), so that what they did by his authority is attributed to him. Robertson catches the imperfect tense, 'he was baptizing,' picturing a continued activity.

The Latin fathers in the Catena raise the doctrinal question: why baptize at all? Their answer, with Augustine, is that it was necessary the Lord himself should be baptized so that no one might ever think himself at liberty to despise baptism; Bede adds that John here relates what Christ did before the Baptist's imprisonment, a period the other Gospels pass over.

The Reformed Calvin uses the riverside setting to infer the mode: it is probable that John and Christ baptized by plunging the whole body beneath the water, near the confluence of the Jordan; yet he is careful to add that we ought not to make a matter of necessity out of the manner, lest the rite be bound to a ceremony.

The Antiochene Chrysostom lifts a moral from the scene: truth seeks no concealment, avoids no danger, fears no snare, and courts no popularity — Christ went up to the feasts and to the crowds not from ostentation but to teach and to heal.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the practical likeness: our Lord, after entering on his public work, travelled much and removed often, like the patriarchs in their sojournings — an instance of his unwearied industry, many a weary step taken to do good. The voices do not contend; together they set the stage for the dispute over purifying and the Baptist's last great testimony that follow.
23:
The narrative pauses on a place-name, and the witnesses use it for geography, for the mode of baptism, and — in one striking case — for the harmony of the Gospels. The grammatical-historical commentators do the topography. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson all note that Aenon means 'springs' or 'fountains,' and that it was reckoned near the better-known Salim; following Eusebius and Jerome, several place it in the Jordan valley some eight Roman miles south of Scythopolis, though Ellicott records the difficulty that this lies outside Judea (compare John 4:3). They fasten especially on the clause because there was much water there: Clarke and Robertson take it as evidence of the manner of baptism, since the multitudes required abundant water, and the Jewish practice had the candidate stand in the water and then plunge beneath it. Gill simply glosses Aenon as a place of an abundant fountain.

The single Alexandrian voice, Origen, stands quite apart and supplies the verse's outlier. Where the others map the ground, he turns the note into a meditation on the Gospels themselves: the apparent discrepancies among the four must not shake our trust in them as true and written by a divine Spirit; rather, those who will not solve the difficulty 'anagogically' — by spiritual, mystical interpretation — are left to clear it up as best they can, for the truth of these matters lies in what is seen by the mind. It is a characteristically Alexandrian move, reading even a question of place toward a deeper, figurative sense.

Barnes adds the historical detail from Eusebius — Aenon some eight Roman miles south of Scythopolis — and observes that John's preaching still drew the multitudes who needed much water for their baptizing, evidence that the forerunner laboured on even after the Messiah had appeared.

The Wesleyan Wesley draws the pastoral point that anticipates the Baptist's coming testimony: John did not repel those who offered themselves, but the more willingly referred them to Jesus — a forerunner content to lose his hearers to the One he announced. The voices do not divide; the chapter is quietly preparing, through this notice of John's continuing work, for the question about purifying in v.25 and the great self-effacement of v.30.
24:
This brief parenthesis carries more weight than its length suggests, and the witnesses — here only the grammatical-historical commentators with one Reformed voice — read it as a deliberate chronological key. Robertson parses the grammar exactly: the periphrastic past perfect, 'John had not yet been cast into prison,' explains by a for why the Baptist was still baptizing at all, the reason for the imprisonment having been given by Luke (Luke 3:19). Ellicott draws the harmonizing inference plainly: this Judean ministry of Jesus, then, preceded the Galilean ministry of the earlier Gospels, which open only after John's arrest (compare John 4:3 and the note on Matthew 4:12). Barnes makes the same point — the mention shows that John was not imprisoned until some time after our Lord entered on his ministry — and turns it into a lesson on perseverance.

The note matters because the Synoptic Gospels begin their account of Jesus' public work with the words 'Now after that John was put in prison' (compare Matthew 4:12); John alone preserves this earlier period in which the ministries of the forerunner and the Messiah overlapped. The evangelist's small remark thus quietly removes an apparent conflict among the Gospels, locating the events of this chapter in a window the others omit, and assuring the reader that the two baptisms ran side by side for a time without rivalry.

The Reformed Gill fills in the sequel the verse anticipates: John was afterward cast into prison by Herod for the sake of Herodias, because he reproved Herod for taking his brother Philip's wife; and this circumstance shows that the things here related happened before the journey of Christ into Galilee that the other evangelists describe. Barnes adds the devotional lesson the verse yields: a minister of religion should be industrious to the day of his death, for John still toiled in his work, labouring no less because the Messiah had come. There is no debate among so few and so agreed a company; the verse stands as the evangelist's careful timestamp, harmonizing the Gospels and honouring the Baptist's faithfulness to the end, which his disciples' complaint in v.26 will soon test.
25:
A sudden controversy breaks out, and the witnesses read it as a quarrel over external rites that masks a deeper insecurity. The grammatical-historical commentators settle the text and the subject. Robertson and Clarke note that the better manuscripts read a Jew rather than 'the Jews,' and Barnes adds that this person was commonly supposed to have been baptized by Jesus' disciples, which gave the dispute its spark. Robertson defines the word for the controversy as a meticulous wrangle (1 Timothy 6:4), and recalls that the Sanhedrin had already challenged John's right to baptize (John 1:25); the Jews practised various washings, so the comparative value of these baptisms was a live question. Ellicott frames it as one of those questions about outward forms that arise in every age and are too often accompanied by a neglect of inner principle, while Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic idiom of 'inquiring' that lies behind the word.

The Reformed Calvin reads the episode as a study in misplaced zeal. It was not without reason, he says, that the evangelist records how the dispute arose among John's disciples; for just in proportion as they were ill-informed about doctrine, they were the more eager to enter into debate — ignorance is always bold and presumptuous. Had others attacked them they might be excused, but to provoke the contest themselves, while unfit to maintain it, was a rash and foolish proceeding. The lesson is pointed: those least grounded in the truth are often quickest to quarrel about its trappings.

The Wesleyan Wesley reads the scene concretely: the men of Judea who now went to be baptized by Jesus, and John's disciples who were mostly of Galilee, disputed over purifying — that is, over baptism, and specifically over which of the two they should be baptized by. The voices do not divide; together they expose how a contest over the sign betrays the disputants' want of grasp on the thing signified, and how easily attachment to a teacher curdles into jealousy. This sets the stage for the disciples' complaint to John in v.26 and for the Baptist's serene and self-forgetting reply.
26:
The dispute over purifying drives John's disciples to their master with a complaint, and the witnesses read it as a portrait of partisan jealousy. The grammatical-historical commentators catch its tone. Barnes describes them coming envious and jealous at the success of Jesus, irritated by the discussion, and fearful that their master was about to be eclipsed and forsaken; the heart of their grievance is simply that all men come to him. Robertson notices a revealing detail — they cannot bring themselves to name Jesus, speaking only of 'he that was with thee beyond Jordan, to whom thou hast borne witness' (compare John 1:29; John 1:34); they are clearly jealous of him as a rival and even blame John for having endorsed the man now overtaking him in popularity. Ellicott reads the same natural attachment to their teacher, untaught as to what that teacher's work really was: the Baptist had borne witness to Jesus, and now Jesus seems to usurp his place, the throngs that had crowded to the forerunner streaming to him instead.

The Reformed Calvin exposes the false logic beneath the complaint. By their argument they endeavour either to make Christ inferior to John or to suggest that John, by honouring him, had laid him under obligation — as if the Baptist had conferred a favour on Christ by adorning him with such honourable titles. Nothing, Calvin replies, could be more unreasonable: it was John's very duty, indeed his highest dignity, to be the herald of the Son of God; to reckon Christ the lesser because the forerunner's testimony was favourable is to invert the whole order of things.

So the chapter sets the disciples' wounded partisanship against the witness they had themselves heard. They remembered that John bore witness to Jesus, as v.28 will recall, but had forgotten what that witness meant; their loyalty to a man had blinded them to the One the man was sent to proclaim. The voices do not divide. Together they prepare for the Baptist's answer, in which he will refuse their jealousy outright and rejoice, as the bridegroom's friend, to decrease that Christ may increase.
27:
The Baptist's reply opens with a maxim the witnesses read in several directions, though all draw from it the same humility. The grammatical-historical commentators divide over its reference. Ellicott lays out the options and favours applying the words to Christ: 'His work, with its influence over men, ought to convince you that his mission is divine' — the immediate connection with v.26 pointing that way. Robertson parses the perfect passive subjunctive, 'unless it be granted him from heaven,' and compares the same thought in 1 Corinthians 4:7. Barnes generalizes it: all success is from heaven — John's and Jesus' alike — so there is no ground for envy. Lightfoot offers a third reading, taking receive as 'perceive,' so that John rebukes his disciples' own dullness: you yourselves prove that no man can apprehend or believe unless it be given him from heaven.

The Antiochene Chrysostom reads the Baptist's pastoral tact: on this jealous question John does not rebuke his disciples, for fear they might separate from him and turn to some other school, but answers gently — no wonder Christ does such excellent works and that all come to him, when the One who does it all is God; human efforts are feeble and short-lived, but these are of divine originating.

The Reformed Calvin surveys the same possibilities — whether John commends the honour God has given Christ, which it is vain for them to resist, or breaks out in indignation at his disciples' little progress — and lets the gravity of the saying stand: what is given from heaven is not to be grudged.

The Wesleyan voices apply it most personally to John. Clarke paraphrases: I received my commission and the power to execute it from above; as I took it up at God's command, so I am ready to lay it down when he pleases. Wesley reads it of both men — neither John nor Christ could receive his office but from God — and praises the manner of the answer: where the disciples spoke with jealousy and resentment, John answers 'with sweet composure of spirit.' The debate over the maxim's reference is real, but it resolves into one confession: every gift, office, and success is heaven's to bestow, which is the death of envy and the root of the humility v.30 will crown.
28:
John now turns his disciples back to his own words, and the witnesses read the verse as the forerunner's appeal to a testimony he had never varied. The grammatical-historical commentators ground it in his record. Robertson recalls that John had from the first put Jesus ahead of himself as the Messiah and had openly denied being the Christ (John 1:20; John 1:23), parsing the phrase 'before that one' as simply marking him the forerunner, and 'I am sent' as a periphrastic perfect — a standing commission. Barnes draws out the reasonableness of it: 'You remember that at first I told you I was not the Messiah'; since John came for no other end than to point Jesus out, his disciples ought not to suppose him Christ's superior, and it was only to be expected that the One he heralded would be more successful than his herald. Ellicott presses the irony of their forgetfulness: they remembered (as v.26 shows) that John had borne witness to Jesus, but they had not remembered what that witness meant — that from the first he had known his own work and the greater work to follow, and that the very thing they now resented was the necessary result of the truth he had always declared.

The Reformed Calvin lifts the verse into a principle of ministry. By affirming that he was sent before Christ, John reserves nothing for himself but to be a servant subject to the Head, taking his place among the rest of the Church and not so highly exalted as to obscure the honour of the One he precedes. He is, Calvin says, like the officer sent to prepare the way for a king — his dignity lies wholly in his relation to the coming Lord, not in any standing of his own.

So this short verse turns the disciples' jealousy back upon the plain consistency of their master's preaching. John had never claimed the place they were tempted to claim for him; his whole office was to go before and to give way. The voices do not differ. Together they make the forerunner's self-effacement not a sudden modesty but the steady meaning of his ministry from the beginning — a meaning the bridal imagery of v.29 will now make luminous.
29:
John answers his disciples' jealousy with the tenderest image in the chapter, and the witnesses unfold it with one accord. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the custom. Ellicott and Clarke explain the 'friend of the bridegroom' — the Hebrew shoshbin, the Greek paranymph — who arranged the contract, acted for the bridegroom in the betrothal, and presided over the wedding, his whole honour bound up in the marriage's success; Lightfoot adds the rabbinic rule that the friend stood aside as judge or witness during the nuptial days. Robertson notes that this is the one place in John's Gospel where the familiar marriage imagery appears, and that Jesus himself takes up the bridegroom figure (Matthew 9:15), which Paul (Ephesians 5:23) and the Revelation (Revelation 19:7) develop; the friend stands and listens with joy at the bridegroom's voice.

The Reformed voices identify the bride and guard the figure. Gill takes her of the elect — the particular souls brought to Christ, and at large the whole Church of the firstborn. Calvin draws the ministerial lesson: as a man who marries does not call his friends to share the bride but that they may honour the marriage, so Christ does not call his ministers to win the Church for themselves and claim dominion over her, but to render the marriage more sacred; the servant who would usurp the bridegroom's place betrays his office.

The Latin Augustine, in the Catena, reads the verse christologically: it shows the excellency of Christ's divinity and the humility of the man who earned the title of the Bridegroom's friend, teaching us to distinguish the man who is only man from the Man who is also God — for the Bridegroom is the Lord Jesus, God before all ages, man in the age of the world.

The Wesleyan voices keep the joy in the foreground. Clarke describes the paranymph who served and afterward reconciled differences between husband and wife, and Wesley draws the conclusion that answers the disciples directly: since all men now come to Jesus, it is plain he is the Bridegroom — and the friend who hears him rejoices greatly, 'so far from envying or resenting it.' The voices do not divide; together they turn the disciples' grievance inside out, making the very success they resented the occasion of the forerunner's fullest joy, which the next verse states as a law.
30:
The Baptist's whole answer crystallizes in a single sentence, and the witnesses treat it as the high-water mark of humility. The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the necessity in it. Robertson marks the present infinitives — Christ is to go on growing while John goes on becoming less — and calls these the last recorded words of the forerunner before the despondent question from the dungeon (Matthew 11:2); they are, he says, 'the fulness of religious sacrifice.' Ellicott returns to the wedding figure: the office of the paranymph ceases to exist once the marriage is accomplished, and so it must be here — John is filled not with disappointment that his work pales before the brightness to follow, but with the joy of having done it. Barnes adds the image of the morning star: his teaching must fade as the light of the morning star is lost in the beams of the rising sun, for he came to point men to Christ, and when that is done his work is done.

The Reformed Calvin gives the theological reason for the decrease. John had been raised by the Lord to the highest dignity, but only for a time; now that the Sun of righteousness has arisen (Malachi 4:2), he must give way — and he is careful not only to scatter the empty honours rashly heaped on him by men, but to guard that even the true honour God had given him should not obscure the glory of Christ.

The Wesleyan voices draw the verse toward every servant of God. Clarke reads it of the ministries: John's baptism and witness, pointing to the coming Messiah, must now cease because the Messiah has come and entered publicly on his work. Wesley turns it into a meditation on succession: those who are now, like John, burning and shining lights must, if not suddenly eclipsed, gradually decrease while others increase about them, as they themselves once rose amid the decays of a former generation — 'let us know how to set, as well as how to rise.' The voices do not contend. Together they make this confession the pattern of the Christian life, the forerunner's gladness in his own decline measuring the greatness of the One he announced, whom the closing verses now exalt above all.
31:
The chapter's closing meditation begins by setting Christ infinitely above every earthly teacher, and the witnesses first ask whose voice now speaks. The grammatical-historical commentators note the question and read the contrast. Barnes thinks it more probable that the Baptist continues, though he holds the point of little consequence; Robertson and Wesley judge that the evangelist now adds his own comment on the words just spoken in v.30. Either way, the sense is one. Ellicott preserves the threefold 'of the earth' the older versions blur — 'he who is of the earth, of the earth he is, and of the earth he speaketh' — marking the Baptist's origin, nature, and speech as wholly earthly over against the One who comes from above. Lightfoot insists the antithesis is not so much between Christ and John as between Christ and all mankind: he is above all degree, while every other is only of earthly rank. Robertson notes John's fondness for ek to mark origin and character.

The Reformed voices ground the supremacy in the Incarnation. Gill explains that Christ came from heaven not by bringing a celestial body nor by change of place — for as God he is immense and infinite — but by the assumption of human nature, in which he came to do the Father's will and the work of our salvation. Calvin draws the comparison of rank: Christ speaks, as it were, from a lofty throne with the authority of a king, while it is enough for John to speak from the lowest footstool beneath him.

The Antiochene Chrysostom reads the verse as John's long, patient cure for the vainglory still clinging to his disciples — a fault he likens to a worm that gnaws the wood from which it springs. They extol his testimony above the One he testifies to; he replies that He who comes from heaven cannot be accredited by an earthly witness, being perfect in himself and above all comparison.

The Wesleyan Clarke ranks the persons plainly: this Bridegroom descended from heaven is above all — above Moses, the prophets, and John — while the merely earthly man can speak only of earthly things and represent divine matters by earthly ordinances. The debate over the speaker is real but slight; on the substance the voices are one — Christ's heavenly origin sets him above all, and the witness that follows in v.32 is the witness of one who has seen and heard the things of God.
32:
The witnesses read this verse as the supreme qualification of the heavenly Witness joined to the sad smallness of his reception, and they agree closely. The grammatical-historical commentators handle the apparent overstatement first. The clause no man receiveth his testimony cannot be pressed literally, for crowds were coming to Jesus; Barnes takes no man in the sense of few — though his doctrine is pure, plain, and sublime, comparatively few received it in faith, many being drawn by lower motives (John 6:26) yet falling away. Robertson agrees it is the rhetorical 'no one,' not to be pressed, since the crowds were superficial as time would show (John 1:11; John 2:24). Ellicott fixes the dignity of the testimony: divine in origin, divine in nature, Christ is divine in teaching too, and that teaching is a witness of things actually seen and heard — a message brought from the Father's home by the Son himself.

The Reformed voices stress the certainty of the witness. Calvin reads what he hath seen and heard as set against doubtful opinions, unfounded rumours, and every kind of falsehood: Christ teaches nothing but what has been fully ascertained, having been taught by the Father, so that he brings forward nothing of his own invention. Gill takes the seeing and hearing to express the clear and perfect knowledge Christ has of all the Father's mind and will, having all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge in him.

The Antiochene Chrysostom reads the verse as John guarding against a false inference: lest any suppose the testimony false because only a few for the present believed, he says no man — meaning only a few — alluding to the unbelief of his own disciples and the insensibility of the Jews, of whom the Prologue had said, 'He came unto his own, and his own received him not.' The Latin Augustine, in the Catena, draws the darker line: there is a people reserved for the wrath of God who do not receive him, set over against those given to the Son.

The voices do not divide. Together they hold two truths in tension — that the witness of Christ is the most certain there can be, since he reports what he has seen in heaven, and that its reception, then as now, is the work of the few, which only deepens the wonder of those who do receive it, as v.33 goes on to say.
33:
Against the unbelief of the many, this verse holds up the believing few, and the witnesses fasten on its arresting image of the seal. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the metaphor. Robertson points to the verb esphragisen, the same figure of sealing used for divine attestation (John 6:27): the one who accepts the witness of Jesus thereby attests that Jesus speaks the message of God. Barnes draws out the legal force — to seal an instrument is to make it sure, to acknowledge it as ours and pledge our veracity that it is binding, as a man seals a bond or a will; so believing a doctrine in the heart is expressed by sealing it, vouching for the truthfulness of God who has spoken it. Ellicott gives the verse a face: earlier disciples such as Andrew and John had passed from the forerunner to the Great Teacher, had heard in his words that which spoke to the divine in their own spirits, and had come away with the conviction 'We have found the Messias' — they received the witness, and so themselves became witnesses, each setting his private seal as the Eastern stamp affixed to a document.

The Reformed voices read the verse as encouragement to a small and tempted flock. Calvin takes it as an exhortation to embrace the Gospel boldly, with no reason to be ashamed or uneasy on account of their small number, since they have God as the Author of their faith, who alone abundantly supplies the place of all the rest; though the whole world should withhold faith, this ought not to prevent good men from giving their assent to God, for they rest on something sure when they know that to believe is to seal that God is true. Gill makes the same point for the encouragement of the few who did receive Christ as the Messiah.

The Wesleyan voices supply the custom behind the figure. Wesley notes that it was usual among the Jews for a witness to set his seal to the testimony he had given, and Clarke applies it to himself as the Baptist — by taking up and proclaiming Christ's testimony he has fully confirmed it, as a testator's seal confirms his instrument. The voices do not divide; together they make faith no mere opinion but a solemn attestation, the believer's signature underwriting the veracity of God — and they pass straight to the ground of that veracity in v.34, the Son who speaks God's own words.
34:
This verse grounds the truthfulness of Christ's witness in his unmeasured anointing, and the witnesses concentrate on the clause God giveth not the Spirit by measure. The grammatical-historical commentators read it of Christ. Barnes explains that, though Jesus was God as well as man, yet as Mediator God anointed him with the Spirit's influences so as to be completely qualified for his work — not in a small degree, as the prophets were inspired for particular occasions and special messages, but fully and continually, the Spirit dwelling in him without limit. Robertson states it sharply: God has put no limit to the Spirit's relation to the Son, giving the Holy Spirit in his fullness to Christ and to no one else in that sense. Clarke calls him the most perfect of all teachers, having received the Spirit as none before him ever did — not for a particular time or people, but in reference to all eternity.

The Reformed voices weigh the grammar more deliberately. Calvin records that the passage is explained two ways: some extend it to the ordinary dispensation, that God, the inexhaustible fountain of all benefits, does not give his Spirit grudgingly or by measure to his people; others, with the stronger claim, refer it to Christ, in whom the Spirit dwells in fullness. Either way Calvin presses the verse's main point — that when we receive the doctrine of Christ we have to do with God himself, for Christ proceeded from none other than the Heavenly Father, and so it is God alone who speaks to us by him. Gill takes the words straightforwardly of Christ, abundantly qualified by the Spirit with which he was anointed.

The Wesleyan Wesley sides with the christological reading without hesitation: God gives him the Spirit not by measure, as he did to the prophets, but immeasurably, so that he speaks the words of God in the most perfect manner. The debate, then, is narrow — whether the immeasurable gift is read first of Christ (the majority) or, on one of Calvin's options, of God's generous giving to his people — but it resolves in the same direction. Because the Spirit rests on the Son without limit, his words are nothing less than the words of God, which is why the seal of v.33 rightly attests that God is true, and why v.35 can say the Father has given all things into his hand.
35:
The closing argument now rises to its ground in the Godhead: the Father loves the Son and has given all things into his hand. The Reformed voices dwell on the relation and the love. Gill insists the relation of Father and Son is natural and necessary, not constituted by appointment or dependent on will: the second person is begotten of the first, of the same nature, equally divine, and this relation existed from all eternity. Calvin asks whether the Father's love for the Son implies he regards all others with hatred, and answers that the love spoken of is not the common love with which God regards his creatures, but that peculiar love which, beginning with the Son, flows from him to all creatures — the love with which, embracing the Son, the Father embraces us also in him, and so is moved to communicate all his benefits to us by the Son's hand.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the gift as universal dominion. Barnes glosses 'hath given all things into his hand' by the great commission's claim of all authority (Matthew 28:18), and notes that the Father loves the Son eminently, above all the prophets and messengers of God. Robertson observes that John makes the same statement elsewhere (John 13:3) and that Jesus makes the identical claim for himself (Matthew 11:27); Ellicott calls the words a striking instance of distinctly Johannine thought and diction appearing already in the earlier Gospels, and points forward to John 5:20, where they meet us again. Clarke draws the contrast with the prophets: they had various gifts — visions, dreams, teaching, comfort — but none possessed all; Christ alone holds their plenitude and is all things in all.

The Antiochene Chrysostom, still tracing the Baptist's long cure for his disciples' vainglory, draws the lesson of humility: as in human arts and the building of cities we come to perfection little by little, so in spiritual things great is the power of this lowly wisdom, by which men are gently led on from error to truth. The voices do not divide; together they make the Father's love for the Son the fountain of every blessing the believer receives, all of it conveyed through the hand into which all things have been given — which is why the verdict of v.36 turns wholly on the believer's relation to that Son.
36:
The chapter ends on a verdict, and the witnesses read it as the final parting of two ways: present life for the believer, abiding wrath for the disobedient. The grammatical-historical commentators fasten on the precise opposite of faith. Ellicott, Robertson, and Clarke all note that the word for the unbeliever — apeitheo — means 'obeyeth not,' not the same word as 'believeth' at the verse's head; it shows that the faith intended is the subjection of the will to the Son, into whose hand the Father has given all things (compare 'the obedience to the faith,' Romans 1:5). The life, they observe, is a present possession — hath eternal life, here and now and for eternity — while the wrath of the unbeliever does not merely await him but abideth, a continuing state already in force. Barnes describes that life as begun here, the first breathings of the soul for immortality, faint at first yet eternal in nature and to be matured in the bliss of heaven.

The Reformed voices ground both clauses in Christ. Calvin reads the verse to teach not only that we are to seek all good things from Christ but the manner of enjoying them: enjoyment consists in faith, since by it we possess Christ, who brings righteousness and the life that is its fruit; when faith is declared the cause of life, we learn that life is found in Christ alone, and in no other way do we become partakers of it. Gill takes the faith here to be the faith of God's elect — not a temporary or merely historical assent, but the gift of God by which a man sees the Son, goes to him, ventures upon him, and commits himself to him, expecting life and salvation from him.

The Wesleyan Wesley reads the present tense warmly: he that believes on the Son hath everlasting life — he has it already, for he loves God, and love is the essence of heaven; while disobedience is named as the consequence of not believing. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem gathers the whole verse into the creed: he that believes on the Son hath everlasting life, he that believes not shall not see life, but the wrath of God abideth on him. The voices do not divide. Together they make this closing word the seal of the entire discourse — that the new birth of v.3 and the gift of v.16 come to a man only through a living, obedient faith in the Son, apart from which the wrath already resting on the world remains.
